methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $96M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 3 same-sector + same-stage peers in Climate & Energy. Range: $110M - $200M.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

private.name: Graphyte
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $200M

private.name: Fourth Power
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $150M

private.name: Steady Energy
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $110M

data.narrative:
Arbor Energy is a Series A climate and energy company founded by Brad Hartwig that has raised $96M to date. Valuation analysis draws from a comparable universe of three same-stage, same-sector private peers (Graphyte, Fourth Power, Steady Energy) valued between $110M and $200M, plus a broader set of nine private climate-tech companies and one public comparable (LNZA).

Two valuation methods were applied. Stage-progression analysis uses the $96M total raised as a base proxy and applies a typical 2.0x step-up multiplier to project a Series A-to-next-stage advancement, yielding $192M. Peer-median analysis across the three direct comparables produces a $150M implied valuation, with a range spanning $110M to $200M. The peer set reflects material heterogeneity in stage-equivalent valuations within climate and energy infrastructure.

Triangulation of both methods supports a valuation range of $128M to $221M, with a median implied value of $171M. Key uncertainties include the applicability of the 2.0x stage multiplier given Arbor's specific capital efficiency and competitive position relative to peers, the representativeness of a three-company peer median given sector heterogeneity, and potential selection bias in the comp universe. The $43M spread between low and high estimates reflects these methodological dependencies rather than fundamental business ambiguity.
